name: Water Trail
name_original: Berlangas de Roa y Hoyales de Roa — circular por el Valle del Duero
description: Jog this 6.5-mile (10.5 km) moderate circular route through the Duero Valley, passing historic towns and river scenery.
region_intro: Jogging around Hoyales De Roa offers a chance to explore the gently rolling terrain of this Spanish region. The landscape is characterized by river valleys, such as the Riaza, and open agricultural areas, with routes often passing through or near local villages. Elevation changes are generally modest, making for accessible running experiences. Best jogging routes around Hoyales De Roa The most popular jogging route is Haza y Valle del Rio Riaza — circular desde Fuentecen, a 8.7 miles (14.0 km) trail that takes 1 hour 33 minutes to complete. This circular route explores the valley of the Riaza river. Another top favourite among local runners is Water Trail, a moderate 6.5 miles (10.5 km) path. This route features minimal elevation gain, offering a relatively flat running experience. Local runners also love the Fuentecén – Fuentecén Church loop from Fuentecén, a 5.7 miles (9.2 km) trail leading through the surroundings of Fuentecén, often completed in about 59 minutes. Jogging around Hoyales De Roa is defined by river valleys, open fields, and village loops. The network offers options for different ability levels, with all routes currently rated as moderate. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 25 runners have used komoot to explore Hoyales De Roa's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available around Hoyales De Roa?
A: There are &lt;strong&gt;over 10 dedicated running routes&lt;/strong&gt; around Hoyales De Roa listed on komoot, offering a variety of experiences through the region's river valleys and agricultural landscapes.

Q: What is the typical difficulty level for running trails in Hoyales De Roa?
A: All the running routes currently listed for Hoyales De Roa are rated as &lt;strong&gt;moderate&lt;/strong&gt;. This means they are generally accessible with modest elevation changes, suitable for runners with some experience.

Q: Are there any circular running routes in the Hoyales De Roa area?
A: Yes, many of the routes are circular, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1050140257/haza-y-valle-del-rio-riaza-circular-desde-fuentecen" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Haza y Valle del Rio Riaza — circular desde Fuentecen&lt;/a&gt; is a popular loop that explores the Riaza river valley.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the jogging paths around Hoyales De Roa?
A: The terrain around Hoyales De Roa is characterized by &lt;strong&gt;gently rolling hills, river valleys, and open agricultural areas&lt;/strong&gt;. You'll often find yourself running through or near local villages, with generally modest elevation changes.

Q: Are there any family-friendly running trails in Hoyales De Roa?
A: While all routes are rated moderate, the modest elevation gains and accessible nature of the trails make many suitable for active families. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1050679847/water-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Water Trail&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, features minimal elevation gain, offering a relatively flat experience.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the running routes in Hoyales De Roa?
A: While specific dog-friendly designations are not provided for each route, generally, dogs are welcome on trails in rural areas of Spain, especially if kept on a leash and waste is properly disposed of. Always be mindful of local regulations and agricultural land.

Q: What are some points of interest or landmarks I might see while running near Hoyales De Roa?
A: The region offers a mix of natural beauty and historical sites. While running, you might encounter charming villages and river landscapes. Nearby attractions include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5506198" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Haza and its castle&lt;/a&gt;, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4915397" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Santa María la Real of Aranda de Duero&lt;/a&gt;, which are worth exploring before or after your run.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about the trails in Hoyales De Roa?
A: Runners using komoot often appreciate the varied terrain, which includes river valleys, open fields, and village loops. The network provides options for different ability levels, allowing for enjoyable exploration of the region's landscape.

Q: Are there any running routes that start from Fuentecén?
A: Yes, Fuentecén is a common starting point for several routes. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31457278" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuentecén – Fuentecén Church loop from Fuentecén&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you through the village's surroundings.

Q: What is the best time of year to go jogging in Hoyales De Roa?
A: Given the region's climate, &lt;strong&gt;spring and autumn&lt;/strong&gt; are generally ideal for jogging in Hoyales De Roa. The temperatures are milder, and the landscape is vibrant. Summer can be quite warm, especially midday, while winter might bring cooler temperatures.

Q: Are there any longer running routes for more experienced runners?
A: Yes, for those looking for a longer challenge, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1050140257/haza-y-valle-del-rio-riaza-circular-desde-fuentecen" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Haza y Valle del Rio Riaza — circular desde Fuentecen&lt;/a&gt; covers approximately 14 km (8.7 miles), offering a substantial run through the scenic river valley.

Q: Can I find routes with minimal elevation gain for an easier run?
A: Absolutely. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1050679847/water-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Water Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a great choice, featuring minimal elevation gain over its 10.5 km (6.5 miles), providing a relatively flat and comfortable running experience.
tips: Population of the Burgos Ribera del Duero located halfway between Aranda and Roa, between the Duero and Riaza plains. Its name reveals the origin of its first settlers: Santa María de Valeria de Berlangas.
The town of Hoyales de Roa is located at one end of the province of Burgos and one of the most characteristic elements of the town is the tower of the castle that has the same name as the town.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to jog this circular route?
faqs.answer: The route is approximately 10.5 kilometers (6.5 miles) long and is designed for jogging. Based on komoot's data, it typically takes around 1 hour and 5 minutes to complete. Trail research suggests a similar route might take up to 3 hours for a more leisurely pace, but for jogging, expect it to be quicker.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this jogging trail?
faqs.answer: This trail is rated as moderate for jogging. It requires a good level of fitness but features mostly accessible paths. The elevation gain is minimal, making it suitable for those with some experience in outdoor activities and a reasonable fitness level.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the 'Sendero del Agua'?
faqs.answer: The terrain is primarily 'de campiña' (countryside), characterized by a mix of cultivated fields and lush riparian vegetation. You'll jog alongside the Duero and Riaza rivers, crossing an old railway line. The surface is generally well-maintained and non-technical, as it's 100% cyclable, making it ideal for jogging.

faqs.question: What natural features and wildlife can I expect to see along the way?
faqs.answer: The trail is known as the 'Sendero del Agua' (Water Trail) due to its abundance of water features, including the confluence of the Riaza and Duero rivers and numerous natural springs like 'Los Caños' and 'Las Palomas'. You'll pass through 'gallery forests' with poplars, willows, and wild roses. This rich environment supports diverse birdlife, such as the Red-backed Shrike and the Grey Heron.

faqs.question: What historical landmarks or points of interest are visible on the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers glimpses into the region's history. You'll see the 12th-century Hoyales de Roa Torreón (large tower) from a distance. In Berlangas de Roa, you can observe the 17th-century parish church of San Andrés and the 12th-century Romanesque hermitage of Virgen de los Huertos. The trail also passes remnants of an old railway line and its former station.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for families or those with children?
faqs.answer: Given its moderate difficulty and generally accessible, non-technical surface, the trail can be enjoyable for active families. The varied scenery and historical points of interest offer engaging elements for children. However, its length of 10.5 km might be challenging for very young children, so consider their endurance.

faqs.question: Is the 'Berlangas de Roa and Hoyales de Roa' trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, this trail is generally dog-friendly. As a countryside route, dogs are typically welcome, but it is always recommended to keep them on a leash, especially when passing through cultivated areas or near wildlife, and to clean up after them.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog this route?
faqs.answer: The trail can be enjoyed year-round. However, for jogging, spring and autumn offer the most pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery. Summer can be warm in the Duero Valley, so early mornings or late afternoons are advisable during those months. Winter jogging is also possible, but be prepared for cooler temperatures.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay an entrance fee to access this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no special permits or entrance fees required to jog the 'Berlangas de Roa and Hoyales de Roa — circular through the Duero Valley' trail. It is a public access route.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trail, and is there public transport available?
faqs.answer: You can typically find parking within the villages of Berlangas de Roa or Hoyales de Roa, which serve as convenient starting points for the circular route. Public transport options directly to these small villages might be limited, so checking local bus schedules for routes connecting to larger towns in the Ribera del Duero region is recommended if you plan not to drive.

faqs.question: Does this trail connect with other well-known paths in the area?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route, also known as Sendero PRC-BU-156 or 'Sendero del Agua', incorporates sections of other established trails. Approximately 73% of the route is part of the 'Sendero del Agua' itself. Additionally, it overlaps with about 38% of 'Etapa 10: Aranda de Duero – Roa' and 38% of the 'Ruta del Duero'.
